content:
The preceding comparison of EVA, POE, and EPE under damp heat stress revealed that encapsulant ranking is condition-dependent, yet it treated each polymer as a monolithic material class. Within EVA specifically—the most widely deployed photovoltaic encapsulant—compositional variables such as vinyl acetate (VA) content and the degree of crosslinking exert a profound influence on durability, warranting a dedicated examination of how these formulation parameters modulate damp heat resistance.

The photovoltaic industry has long operated under a general consensus that poly(ethylene-co-vinyl acetate) (PEVA) encapsulant should have VA content between 28 and 33% [REF:11]. This range reflects a historical compromise between optical transmittance, flexibility, and processability. However, systematic damp heat aging experiments across a broader VA range challenge this convention. PEVA formulations with lower VA content—specifically 18% and 24%—demonstrated greater tensile strength, thermal stability, and degree of crosslinking after damp heat aging compared with higher-VA formulations [REF:11]. Conversely, PEVA with higher VA content of 33% and 40% degraded significantly after 2000 hours of damp heat aging, resulting in significant loss in transmittance and mechanical integrity [REF:11]. The divergence is substantial: while lower-VA formulations retained structural cohesion, higher-VA variants suffered coupled optical and mechanical deterioration that would compromise module performance in the field.

The mechanistic basis for this VA-dependent vulnerability lies in the acetate side group itself. Each vinyl acetate unit introduces an ester linkage susceptible to hydrolytic cleavage under the combined action of heat and moisture. At higher VA loadings (33–40%), the density of these hydrolyzable sites increases proportionally, accelerating chain scission, acetic acid generation, and the consequent loss of crosslink network integrity. Lower VA formulations (18–24%) present fewer vulnerable ester bonds per unit chain length, preserving the polyethylene crystalline backbone that contributes to tensile strength and thermal dimensional stability. This structure–property relationship explains why tensile strength, thermal stability, and crosslinking retention were all found to be greater for PEVA containing lower VA content (18% and 24%) and diminished for PEVA with higher VA content (33% and 40%) after damp heat exposure [REF:11].

Despite the superior mechanical retention of the lowest VA formulation (PEVA-18%), practical deployment constraints complicate a straightforward recommendation. The study notes that PEVA with 18% VA content has inadequate transmittance and relatively high stiffness after 2000 hours of damp heat aging [REF:11], which would penalize optical coupling to the photovoltaic cell and increase stress transfer at the encapsulant–cell interface. These trade-offs narrow the optimal VA content window: 24% VA emerges as the formulation that balances mechanical durability against optical and mechanical compliance requirements. Both the detailed aging investigation [REF:11] and a corroborating industry-facing analysis [REF:12] converge on the recommendation to reduce VA content in PEVA to 24% for better encapsulation response against damp heat aging [REF:12][REF:11]. This convergence across independent sources strengthens the case that the prevailing 28–33% industry standard may be suboptimal for long-term field reliability under humid climates.

| VA Content | Tensile Strength after DH | Thermal Stability after DH | Crosslinking Retention | Transmittance after DH | Overall DH Resistance |
|------------|---------------------------|----------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| 18% | Greater [REF:11] | Greater [REF:11] | Greater [REF:11] | Inadequate [REF:11] | Good mechanical, poor optical |
| 24% | Greater [REF:11] | Greater [REF:11] | Greater [REF:11] | Acceptable [REF:11] | Recommended optimum [REF:12] |
| 28–33% | Industry standard [REF:11] | Baseline [REF:11] | Baseline | Baseline | Current convention |
| 33–40% | Significant loss [REF:11] | Significant loss [REF:11] | Diminished [REF:11] | Significant loss [REF:11] | Poor [REF:11] |

The degree of crosslinking—quantified as gel content—represents the second critical formulation axis. Crosslinking stabilizes the EVA network by constraining chain mobility, reducing moisture diffusivity, and distributing mechanical loads across a three-dimensional bonded structure rather than through entanglement alone. However, the relationship between gel content and adhesion durability is nonlinear. There exists a crosslinking saturation level above which gel content influence on adhesion becomes negligible [REF:13], implying that the marginal benefit of additional crosslinking diminishes once the network reaches a percolation threshold. This saturation behavior has practical implications: manufacturers who drive gel content upward beyond the saturation point—sometimes exceeding 92% in pursuit of perceived durability gains—incur processing penalties and risk UV-induced yellowing without meaningful adhesion improvement.

The interaction between VA content and crosslinking degree introduces a coupled optimization problem. Lower VA formulations inherently crosslink more efficiently because the ethylene-rich backbone provides more accessible sites for peroxide-initiated radical crosslinking, and fewer acetate groups means less steric hindrance at potential crosslink junctions. The greater degree of crosslinking observed in lower-VA PEVA after damp heat aging [REF:11] is therefore not merely a survival effect but reflects the intrinsically higher crosslink potential of ethylene-rich compositions. When combined with the crosslinking saturation phenomenon [REF:13], this suggests an optimal formulation space: VA content near 24% [REF:12] with gel content managed to the saturation threshold rather than maximized indiscriminately.

Several methodological limitations bound these findings. The available claims do not report specific gel content percentages corresponding to the saturation level identified [REF:13], making it difficult to prescribe a universal crosslinking target. The VA content studies [REF:11] employ 2000-hour damp heat aging, which, while conforming to standard IEC protocols, represents an accelerated proxy for decades of field exposure whose correlation with real-world degradation kinetics is imperfectly understood. Furthermore, the transmittance and stiffness penalties associated with 18% VA [REF:11] were observed at 2000 hours but their trajectory at intermediate time points is not characterized, leaving open the possibility that PEVA-18% might perform adequately for shorter-lifecycle applications. Quantitative adhesion energy (Gc) measurements as a function of VA content are absent from the current claim set, which limits direct comparison with the Gc-based analyses presented in earlier sections of this review.

Future research would benefit from mapping the gel content saturation threshold [REF:13] across multiple VA compositions to produce a two-dimensional formulation optimization surface. Accelerated aging protocols extending beyond 2000 hours, paired with outdoor exposure validation, would clarify whether the 24% VA recommendation [REF:12] holds under real-world spectral and humidity cycling conditions. Quantitative peel or double cantilever beam adhesion testing as a function of both VA content and gel content would bridge the gap between bulk mechanical properties (tensile strength, thermal stability) and the interfacial adhesion metrics most directly relevant to delamination risk.

**Key Findings**

- PEVA with lower VA content (18% and 24%) retains greater tensile strength, thermal stability, and crosslinking degree after damp heat aging than higher-VA formulations (33–40%), which suffer significant transmittance and mechanical degradation at 2000 hours [REF:11].
- The industry consensus VA range of 28–33% may be suboptimal for humid climates; independent sources converge on 24% VA as the recommended optimum for damp heat resistance [REF:11][REF:12].
- PEVA with 18% VA, despite strong mechanical retention, exhibits inadequate transmittance and high stiffness after damp heat aging, disqualifying it for standard photovoltaic applications [REF:11].
- A crosslinking saturation level exists above which further increases in gel content yield negligible adhesion benefit, indicating that gel content should be managed to a threshold rather than maximized [REF:13].
- The coupled optimization of VA content and crosslinking degree remains incompletely characterized, with quantitative adhesion energy data across the full VA–gel content parameter space representing a critical research gap [REF:11][REF:13].
